text:
We investigated the dynamic light absorption of wood-burning organic carbon (OC) emitted from smoldering fires and photochemically aged in a 104 m3 dual outdoor chamber under simulated ambient sunlight conditions. Using a UV-visible spectrometer with an integrating sphere, we probed the time evolution of OC light absorption while continuously varying relative humidity (RH) and NOx levels over a diel cycle. The mass absorption cross section (MAC; integrated from 280 to 600 nm) consistently showed an increase of 11–54% in the morning due to secondary organic aerosol chromophore production. In the afternoon, photo-oxidation proceeded sufficiently to cause significant sunlight bleaching that led to absorption reductions of 19–68%.

The environmental conditions that influence these optical transitions are elucidated, and the corresponding chemical changes characterized using a variety of spectroscopic techniques. Increasing RH results in faster color loss due to aqueous-phase photochemistry, while $\text{NO}_x$ has a mild positive effect on the optical properties by enabling more chromophoric nitrogen-containing species to form. Characterization of the color change through fluorescence spectroscopy indicated that the fluorescence intensity of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), which is an important contributor to the absorption of BC-containing particles, dropped dramatically over time. Meanwhile, levoglucosan, which is an important indicator species for the primary organic aerosol (POA) of biomass burning, was also observed to decrease in concentration as the particles aged. Using the infrared spectroscopic technique, we found that woodsmoke particles also become less hygroscopic with aging while becoming more oxygenated. These chamber studies of light-induced color change are consistent with the in-situ measurements of ambient aged smoke from the County Line wildfire that occurred in Florida in August 2012. Overall, our chamber studies suggest that the biomass burning organic carbon (OC) in particles is much less light scattering in air after being exposed to sunlight for only around 8–9 hours, thereby suggesting that any radiative forcing due to OC associated with aged biomass burning particles is much lower than expected, and suggesting that more accurate modeling of this component would result in improved atmospheric radiative forcing models.
